text:
Team GBenjoyed a memorable, record-breaking Day 10 at the 2024Olympics, but it was disappointment forSimone Bilesin Paris.

The greatest female gymnast of all time had alreadyclaimed three gold medals at the 2024 Games.

However, Biles was twice denied gold as shemissed out on a medal altogether in the balance beam finalbefore only managing a silver in the floor final.

Later in the evening, Keely Hodgkinson – one of Team GB’s great medal hopes – lived up to expectations, and then some, withgold in a stunning women’s 800m final.

Hodgkinson’s triumph came afteranother gold medal for Team GB in the velodrome, with Katy Marchant, Emma Finucane and Sophie Capewell breaking three world records in three rides to win thewomen’s team sprint.

This blog is now closed. For the latest updates, visitMetro’s Sport page.

That’s day 10 done and dusted at the Paris Olympics and what a day it was…

We’ll be back bright and early here atMetro.co.ukfor yet more drama from the French capital, with a total of 15 gold medals up for grabs.

The women’s 200m final headlines the athletics programme ahead of what promises to be a pulsating night of action at the Stade de France.

Goodnight!

Paula Radcliffe appeared to agree with Faith Kipyegon’s disqualification after a controversial women’s 5,000m Olympic final, in which Beatrice Chebet claimed gold.

Kipyegon – the former 5,000m world-record holder – clashed with Gudaf Tsegay with around two laps remaining at the Stade de France, which resulted in the Kenyan losing her silver medal.

‘To actually grab hold of their arm and move them wide… I think that’s why the DQ has gone in,’ Radcliffe explained on BBC Sport.

Read thefull story here.

Team GB star Keely Hodgkinson said she simply ‘could not believe’ she had achieved her life-long dream after she became just the third British woman in history to win 800m Olympic gold.

Only Dame Kelly Holmes and Ann Packer had claimed top spot in the 800m for Britain prior to Hodkinson’s breathtaking performance in Paris.

After the win, the 22-year-old told BBC Sport: ‘I’m now the Olympic champion for the next four years and nobody can take that away from me.’

Complete and utter superstar.

Read thefull story here.

Swedish superstar Armand Duplantis lit up the Stade de France to win gold in the men’s pole vault final, setting a new world record in the process.

Remarkably, Duplantis’ record of 6.25m is his ninth world record. Yes, his NINTH.

American Sam Kendricks gets the silver and Emmanouil Karalis secures the bronze medal for Greece.

It’s an astonishing gold medal for Great Britain’s Keely Hodgkinson!

Hodgkinson, one of Team GB’s great hopes at this summer’s Games, produced a masterclass in 800m running to clinch gold at the Stade de France.

The 22-year-old dug deep to secure victory and had to hold off a strong challenge at the death from both Tsige Dguma and Mary Moraa, who claimed silver and bronze respectively.

It doesn’t get much better than that…

It’s gold for Kenya’s Beatrice Chepkoech after an absorbing women’s 5,000m final!

Faith Kipyegon held the lead heading into the final bend at the Stade de France, but failed to hold off a stunning late charge from her compatriot and appeared to have finished second.

However, Kipyegon was subsequently disqualified following a mid-race tussle with Gudaf Tsegay, which saw the Netherlands’ Sifan Hassan take silver.

Nadia Battocletti claims the bronze medal for Italy.

There were concerning scenes in Spain’s semi-final victory over Morocco in the men’s football tournament, with the referee having to be replaced due to a nasty-looking injury.

Ilgiz Tantashev of Uzbekistan had been selected to officiate the high-stakes clash but hobbled off the field after only 15 minutes following a heavy collision with Spanish defender Marc Pubill.

Glenn Nyberg, the fourth official, came on to replace Tantashev before Spain mounted a second-half comeback to win 2-1.

Read thefull story here.

Sophie Capewell broke down in tears as she paid tribute to her late father in the aftermath of Team GB’s victory in an historic women’s team sprint final at theParisOlympics.

New Zealand’s trio started the stronger of the two teams before Britain fought back to set an astonishing time of 45.186 seconds in the velodrome, their third world record in as many rides.

After the win, Capewell told BBC Sport: ‘I’ve had my own personal battles and I wish my dad was up there in the stands watching it. He would be super proud of me.’

Yes he would, Sophie ❤️

Read thefull story here.

Dina Asher-Smith was lost for words when she didn’t make the women’s 100m final. She’s redeemed herself tonight, finishing second in her semi-final.

Daryll Neita, who agonisingly missed out on a medal in the 100m, also finishes second in her semi.

Fellow Brit Bianca Williams fails to make the cut however.

After winning Euro 2024 (no, still not over it), Spain could now be crowned Olympic champions.

They came from behind to beat Morocco to win their semi-final 2-1, with Juanlu Sanchez scoring the winner in the 85th minute.

They’ll face either hosts France or Egypt in the men’s final.

That’s 11 golds for Team GB in Paris and there could be more tonight if Keely Hodgkinson wins the 800m tonight.

For now, let’s watch that thrilling cycling race back

Gold medal winning brilliance from@TeamGB’s women’s team sprint! ð¥Katy Marchant, Sophie Capewell and Emma Finucane set a third new world record at#Paris2024.#BBCOlympics#Olympicspic.twitter.com/oM5aJe44QU

Yes! Great Britain’s first ever medal in the women’s team sprint is golden!

As expected, New Zealand started stronger, but on lap two GB raced back to take advantage and there was no stopping them from there.

Victory by nearly half-a-second and a time of 45.186 seconds – ANOTHER WORLD RECORD!!!

They broke the world record in qualifying and then again in the first round – now they race for gold.

Emma Finucane, Sophie Capewell and Katy Marchant have the chance to win Team GB’s first gold of the day and in the velodrome.

Only New Zealand stand in their way…

Team GB’s men’s sprint team – Jack Carlin, Ed Lowe and Hamish Turnbull –  have made a flying start to their campaign.

A time of 41.862 seconds is very impressive – but only second fastest.

Gold medal favourites the Netherlands are quickest and set a new Olympic record in the process. The men’s event continues Tuesday evening.

In all that excitement, it’s important to know that Team GB have just guaranteed themselves another medal.

They’ll take on New Zealand in the final of the women’s team sprint, which starts in 30 minutes’ time.

Great Britain may have just reclaimed the world record but the Kiwis were just 0.01 seconds slower in that round. It’s going to be close…

Earlier today, Team GB broke the world record in the women’s team sprint at the velodrome.

10 minutes ago, Germany broke that record.

5 minutes ago, New Zealand broke that record.

And now, Team GB have the world record again!

A time of 45.338 seconds for Emma Finucane, Sophie Capewell and Katy Marchant. Incredible!

It’s the end of the road for Team GB’s women’s hockey team at this year’s Olympics in Paris.

Britain have slumped to a 3-1 defeat by the Netherlands in the quarter-final.

GB fell behind within 48 seconds after Xan De Waard slammed home but drew level through Hannah French five minutes into the second quarter.

Netherlands re-took the lead on the stroke of half-time through Luna Fokke, despite GB using a referral after claiming a stick tackle in the lead up to the goal.

The decision wasn’t overturned and Fokke scored again in the final quarter to make it 3-1, ending any faint GB hopes of a medal.

Netherlands progress to the semi-final where they will face one of either China, Argentina, Spain or Belgium.

If you saw or read about the gymnastics beam finals earlier today, you’ll know thatUS superstar Simone Biles was pretty hacked off with the crowd.

There were audible gasps at the Bercy Arena after an out-of-sorts Biles fell off the apparatus during an ambitious routine, denying the iconic athlete an eighth gold medal.

The 27-year-old managed to regain her composure before finishing her routine but had to settle for a fifth-placed finish.

But she appeared to shout at the crowd to ‘stop shushing’, before turning to teammate Sunisa Lee and asking: ‘Why are they all shushing?! As soon as I’m going they’re like “shushing” so much.’

She’s now explained in more detail why she was so upset. You canread the full story on that here.

We’re into the final quarter of the women’s hockey quarter-final between Team GB and the Netherlands and it’s not looking good.

The Dutch have just scored another and now lead 3-1 with less than 15 minutes remaining.

GB need to score at least two to salvage a draw and keep their hopes alive.

Chances of a medal are fading…

Wow, you have to tip your hat to the Aussies there.

Australia have gone ahead of Team GB in the men’s pursuit qualifiers.

A lightning quick start was enough to see them finish 0.283 seconds ahead of Britain, who now move down to second place.

Denmark almost knocked GB down another place but have gone into third after finishing just 0.449 seconds slower.

Defending champions Italy were the final team to go and have finished fourth.

The top eight teams advance but finishing in the top four is needed to guarantee a shot at the gold and silver medal spots.

France, New Zealand, Belgium and Canada are the next nations through respectively. Germany and Japan are out.

The heats will take place tomorrow before the finals on Wednesday.

title: Olympics schedule and results: Athletics, gymnastics, cycling, hockey | Metro News
description: Latest news and live updates from the 2024 Paris Olympic Games on day 10's schedule: athletics, gymnastics, cycling and hockey.
